candidate.title: A large-scale dataset of pre- and postsurgical MRI data from patients with chronic trigeminal neuralgia

sources.text: Skip to main content Thank you for visiting nature.com. You are using a browser version with limited support for CSS. Advertisement View all journals Search Account Log in Content Explore content About the journal Publish with us Sign up for alerts RSS feed nature scientific data data descriptors article A large-scale dataset of pre- and postsurgical MRI data from patients with chronic trigeminal neuralgia Download PDF Download PDF Data Descriptor Open access Published: 12 December 2025 A large-scale dataset of pre- and postsurgical MRI data from patients with chronic trigeminal neuralgia Elena Filimonova 1 , 2 na1 , Anton Pashkov 1 , 2 , 3 na1 , Anastasiia Tiutiunnik 4 , Galina Moysak 1 , 2 , 5 , Azniv Martirosyan 1 , Vladimir Kurilov ORCID: orcid.org/0009-0004-8423-9533 3 , Ivan Brak 4 & … Jamil Rzaev 1 , 2 , 5 Show authors Scientific Data volume 12 , Article number: 2024 ( 2025 ) Cite this article 3107 Accesses 4 Citations Metrics details Subjects Diagnostic markers Prognostic markers Abstract The study of chronic pain is a priority in contemporary medical science because of its significant social importance. In this article, we present a large dataset of neuroimaging data from patients with chronic trigeminal neuralgia (112 patients and 48 healthy subjects). The majority of patients underwent surgical treatment either in the form of microvascular decompression or percutaneous interventions (radiofrequency rhizotomy and balloon compression). The remaining patients were observed on an outpatient basis at the neurosurgical hospital (n = 9). Follow-up data six months after treatment were available for 58 patients. In addition to the MRI data, the dataset consists of clinical and demographic information about the patients, including the type, intensity, and side of pain; duration of the disease; and data on pharmacological treatment. By utilizing this dataset, researchers have the opportunity to conduct a detailed examination of the morphometric, tractographic, and functional changes in the brains of patients, allowing them to independently define the focus of their specific research interests. Similar content being viewed by others A multi-site, multi-disorder resting-state magnetic resonance image database Article Open access 30 August 2021 A novel indicator to predict the outcome of percutaneous stereotactic radiofrequency rhizotomy for trigeminal neuralgia patients: diffusivity metrics of MR-DTI Article Open access 22 April 2024 Trigeminal neuralgia Article 30 May 2024 Background & Summary Chronic pain is a significant challenge for healthcare systems globally and profoundly impacts the quality of life of individuals affected by these conditions. Orofacial pain syndromes, in particular, are often reported as being particularly intolerant for patients, as they can severely hinder essential daily activities such as washing one’s face, brushing one’s teeth, speaking, and consuming food and liquids. Considerable efforts by the clinical and scientific community have recently focused on enhancing diagnostics and, ultimately, the treatment of these conditions. This is achieved primarily through the identification of specific biomarkers that can aid in the development of patient-oriented, personalized approaches. Many of these approaches rely on MRI-derived biomarkers that indicate the pathophysiological processes involved in the development and maintenance of the disease. Previous studies conducted over the past few decades have been successful in delineating structural and functional brain alterations in patients with chronic facial pain 1 , 2 , 3 . Specifically, several studies investigating neural changes in patients with chronic trigeminal neuralgia have identified a range of variables that effectively predict treatment outcomes 4 , 5 , 6 . A different approach to characterize the pathogenesis of chronic pain and treatment resistance involves considering the contributions of neurocognitive performance scores and personality variables. These factors have been shown to effectively complement clinical assessments and neuroimaging data in prognosticating treatment outcomes 7 , 8 . However, when considered collectively, these studies frequently suffer from limited statistical power due to the use of small patient samples, which constrains the reliability and validity of the findings. Additionally, these studies often concentrate on a narrow range of selected measures, which serve as the primary focus of the investigation (e.g., diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) or functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) data). In this study, we provide a comprehensive dataset comprising high-resolution neuroimaging data collected from 112 patients with primary trigeminal neuralgia (TN) alongside 48 age- and sex-matched healthy control subjects. The data from 58 patients were also available at the 6-month follow-up after surgery. This dataset represents an intermediate outcome of the ongoing project designed to identify novel and promising biomarkers across multiple levels, from biochemical to psychological. The ultimate objective is to improve long-term clinical decision-making through the integration of a substantial amount of patient-specific multimodal data. On the basis of this dataset, our research team has recently published several papers that highlight notable differences between the patient group and the control group. Specifically, Filimonova and colleagues demonstrated that patients with trigeminal neuralgia exhibited a significant reduction in fractional anisotropy (FA) values in both hemispheres compared with the control group. This reduction in FA was particularly evident in areas including the corona radiata, internal capsule, optic radiation, and thalami, as well as in certain structures located in the posterior fossa, especially the cerebellar peduncles 9 . Using a recently developed MRI-based method to measure the macromolecular proton fraction (MPF), the same research team reported a statistically significant reduction in the average MPF of the affected trigeminal nerve root in patients with trigeminal neuralgia (TN) compared with healthy controls. Importantly, MPF values were decreased in both the root entry zone (REZ) and the central cisternal regions of the affected nerve. Additionally, TN patients demonstrated lower average MPF values, as well as lower REZ MPF values, on the affected side than on the contralateral side 10 . Another study by Pashkov and colleagues revealed notable changes in gray matter volume within the thalamic nuclei of patients suffering from trigeminal neuralgia. Significant alterations were observed in the intralaminar nuclei (centromedian/parafascicular) and the nuclei related to visual and auditory signal processing (lateral and medial geniculate bodies), in contrast to the ventral group of nuclei, which are involved in nociceptive processing. Furthermore, our findings indicated that there was no significant increase in volume in any of the examined nuclei six months after successful surgical intervention 11 . The provided dataset has the potential to be instrumental in addressing a wide range of clinical issues related to chronic pain. A detailed analysis of this dataset may enable the identification of differences between patients with trigeminal neuralgia caused by vascular compression and those with idiopathic neuralgia, for whom no specific cause has been identified. Additionally, the dataset can be utilized to conduct a comparative analysis of pre- and postoperative changes observed through neuroimaging in patients, depending on the surgical outcome. Alternatively, researchers may also wish to explore the neuroimaging findings associated with paroxysmal pain, commonly referred to as type 1 trigeminal neuralgia, in comparison with persistent pain, known as type 2 TN. These research directions are not intended to be an exhaustive list of potentially relevant clinical questions and can be readily expanded by clinicians or scientists who wish to explore additional topics. Methods Participants and study design This prospective study was carried out at our hospital from October 2021 to October 2024 and is part of an ongoing scientific initiative aimed at thoroughly examining neuroimaging, neuropsychological, and blood biomarkers associated with chronic facial pain, as well as identifying predictors of surgical outcomes. The study included a total of 160 participants after all the exclusion criteria were applied. Among these patients, 103 patients were diagnosed with trigeminal neuralgia and referred for surgical intervention due to the ineffectiveness of their medication or the presence of significant side effects (ages 30–84, 56% female). Additionally, nine patients receiving conservative outpatient management at our center were included (ages 39–71, 89% female). Sex- and age-matched healthy controls (n = 48, ages 38–75, 55% female) were recruited through local advertisements aimed at hospital staff. The study included patients with constant or episodic unilateral or bilateral pain in the trigeminal nerve innervation zone, who had no MRI contraindications, no intracranial or facial anomalies, and no other neurological or psychiatric disorders. The participants had MMSE scores above 24 and were right-handed 12 . All patients diagnosed with classical or idiopathic trigeminal neuralgia who met the criteria from the International Classification of Headache Disorders and patients with secondary trigeminal neuralgia were excluded to reduce confounding factors 13 . The most common comorbid medical conditions in the study sample were hypertension (68%), gastropathy (62%) and obesity (33%). The study excluded participants who had additional chronic pain conditions. The overall design of the project is summarized in Fig. 1 . During the outpatient phase, all patients were required to complete a set of psychological questionnaires 2 to 4 weeks prior to their admission. On the first day of their hospital stay, patients underwent MRI examination in accordance with a developed protocol. Additionally, a neuropsychological assessment was conducted on the same day. At the same time, an assessment of the severity of pain syndrome was carried out via the Brief Pain Inventory (BPI-facial) 14 . The dataset contains only data on the average pain levels of patients during the week preceding hospital admission. Blood samples were collected from patients on the day of surgery, which was the second day of their hospital stay, prior to the commencement of the procedure (not reported in the dataset). Fig. 1 Full size image General design of the project. The follow-up data of patients were collected after the surgical intervention, following the same examination protocol as for presurgical assessment (mean follow-up time = 214.8 days, std = 43.3). The design of the project did not include the collection of follow-up data for patients being treated in the outpatient setting. All patients and healthy controls provided written informed consent to participate in the study, in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki. In addition to obtaining consent for participation, all participants provided explicit consent for data sharing as part of this study. Ethics approval for the research protocol was given by the local ethics committee of the Federal Center of Neurosurgery, dated 25.05.21 (protocol # 7). MRI acquisition and data quality check MR imaging data were acquired via a 3 T system (Ingenia, Philips Healthcare, The Netherlands) equipped with a 16-channel receiver head coil. Imaging was conducted according to a multiparametric high-resolution brain MRI protocol, including the following: 1. High-resolution 3D T1-weighted gradient echo imaging with the following acquisition parameters: TR, 6.57 msec; TE, 2.95 msec; and flip angle, 8 degrees. Images were obtained in the sagittal plane with a nominal voxel size of 0.5 × 0.5 × 0.5 mm 3 after interpolation (matrix 256 × 256 × 340, field-of-view 256 × 256 × 170 mm), with no partial Fourier transform. The actual voxel size was 1 × 1 × 1 mm 3 . The protocol included 2 runs for each subject, and the scanning time was 5 min per run. 2. High-resolution 3D T2-weighted turbo spin echo imaging with the following acquisition parameters: TR, 3500 msec; TE, 300 msec; and flip angle, 90 degrees. Images were obtained in the sagittal plane with a nominal voxel size of 0.5 × 0.5 × 0.5 mm 3 after interpolation (matrix 256 × 256 × 360, field-of-view 256 × 256 × 180 mm), with no partial Fourier transform. The actual voxel size was 1 × 1 × 1 mm 3 . The scanning time was 5 min 18 sec. 3. Diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) with the following acquisition parameters: TR - 12 sec, TE - 110 msec, 64 diffusion directions with b = 1500 and one volume with b = 0, phase encoded direction anterior to posterior. Images were obtained in the axial plane with an actual voxel size of 2 × 2 × 2 mm 3 without interpolation (matrix 128 × 126 × 144, field-of-view 256 × 256 × 144 mm, covering the entire brain), with partial Fourier transform. Additionally, three short sequences were obtained with b = 0 only (one with a phase-encoded direction anterior to posterior and two with a phase-encoded direction posterior to anterior) because they are necessary for subsequent spatial distortion correction. The total scanning time was 20 min. 4. Resting-state functional MRI (rs-fMRI) was conducted with the following parameters: EPI-BOLD sequence, TR – 3 sec, TE – 30 msec, flip angle – 90 degrees, 200 volumes (plus 10 dummy scans), and eyes closed. Images were obtained in the axial plane with an actual voxel size of 3 × 3 × 3 mm 3 without interpolation (matrix 80 × 80×, field-of-view 240 × 240 × 135 mm, covering the entire brain), with partial Fourier transform. The scanning time was 10 min. An example of the project’s MRI data is presented in Fig. 2 . Additionally, conventional sequences, including FLAIR, DWI, SWI, 3D-TOF, and postcontrast T1-weighted imaging (not presented here), were obtained to exclude gross focal brain pathologies and to verify compliance with the inclusion and exclusion criteria. Fig. 2 Full size image Examples of defaced structural (panel A for T1w and panel B for T2w), DWI (panel C ) and functional images (panel D ). Initially, conventional MRI data were analyzed by a neuroradiologist to identify additional pathologies not related to disease (such as prominent signs of small vessel disease, for example). Furthermore, cerebellopontine angle structures were visually evaluated with 3D T2 FFE and 3D TOF, as well as postcontrast T1w sequences in each case, for the assessment of the presence and severity of neurovascular conflict, according to the Sindou classification, as previously described 15 . The main findings for each patient are provided in the “participants.tsv” file. In the second step, high-resolution structural images (both T1w and T2w) were visually evaluated by a neuroradiologist to exclude series with prominent artifacts (due to motion, stomatological implants, etc.). After that, the whole dataset was reorganized into a BIDS ( https://bids.neuroimaging.io/ ) with dcm2bids software ( https://github.com/UNFmontreal/Dcm2Bids ). Structural series (T1w and T2w) were defaced with Pydeface software ( https://github.com/poldracklab/pydeface ). The acquired structural MR images were subsequently subjected to a quality assessment via the MRI-QC package - https://mriqc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ . Pain assessment and surgical interventions The intensity of a patient’s pain was evaluated at the time of hospital admission and again during a six-month follow-up via the BPI-facial tool. This assessment tool employs an 11-point numerical rating scale (0–10), enabling patients to report their minimum, maximum, and average pain intensity experienced over the preceding week. Additionally, the BPI examines the impact of pain on various aspects of a patient’s daily life and helps identify significant pain characteristics, including sensations such as tingling, burning, or numbness. The type of surgery was chosen on the basis of the combination of clinical and neuroimaging data. Patients with neurovascular conflict identified via MRI received microvascular decompression, a safe and effective treatment for pharmacoresistant trigeminal neuralgia. Patients without neurovascular conflict (idiopathic TN) were treated with either radiofrequency rhizotomy, which uses heat to destroy pain-causing trigeminal nerve fibers, or balloon compression, which involves inserting a balloon into the affected area. On the third to fourth days of the patient’s hospital stay, an evaluation of surgical complications was performed via the Theodosopoulos scale (details not provided) 16 . This scale categorizes all neurosurgical complications into two distinct groups: minor (for example, allergic reactions or deep venous thrombosis) and major (CSF leakage, postoperative hemorrhage, etc). The surgical outcome was classified as favorable or unfavorable on the basis of the extent of pain reduction following the procedure. Only 100% pain reduction after surgery was considered a favorable outcome. Data Records The dataset is freely available on the open access OpenNeuro platform in BIDS format and includes all the required components 17 . Clinical and demographic information for each participant, along with primary MRI findings, neurovascular compression grade (according to the Sindou classification, if suitable), and surgical procedure type are provided in the “participants.tsv” file. The variables are detailed in the “ participants.json” file. The entire dataset includes structural and functional MRI volumes for 112 patients with PTN, organized in folders labeled sub-***, and 48 healthy volunteers in folders labeled sub-**. Furthermore, 6-month follow-up MRI data are available for 58 patients stored in folders labeled sub-***fu. Each participant’s directory has the following folders: - the “anat” folder contains high-resolution defaced structural images (T1w and T2w) suitable for automatic segmentation with FreeSurfer ( https://surfer.nmr.mgh.harvard.edu/ ) or alternative software; - the “func” folder includes functional resting-state BOLD-EPI MRI data (4D data with 200 volumes per participant, excluding dummy scans); - the “dwi” folder contains diffusion tensor imaging data with 64 diffusion directions (*_run-01.nii.gz file) and three b0-only runs with both anterior to posterior (*_run-04.nii.gz) and posterior to anterior (*_run-02.nii.gz and *_run-03.nii.gz) phase-encoded directions relative to the main sequence, acquired for spatial distortion correction with the FSL TOPUP procedure ( https://web.mit.edu/fsl_v5.0.10/fsl/doc/wiki/topup.html ) or alternative software. Figure 3 shows the data structure. Further details about our dataset can be found in the “README.txt” file. Fig. 3 Full size image Structure of the dataset shown for one participant. Technical Validation The MR images were analyzed via MRIQC software ( https://github.com/nipreps/mriqc ) to obtain various image quality metrics. Metrics such as the coefficient of joint variation (CJV), contrast-to-noise ratio (CNR), signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and others were calculated for structural images (Fig. 5 ); the entropy-focus criterion (EFC), FBER, FWHM, SNR, temporal SNR, and others were calculated for functional images (Fig. 6 ). The MRIQC reports for the fMRI data were examined for any noticeable artifacts, including aliasing, distortion, and ghosting. The outputs of the whole dataset MRIQC metrics are available in the “mriqc_data” folder (in.tsv and.html formats). To ensure that the current group sizes provide sufficient statistical power to detect sign
